title: Cream puffs
subtitle: Choux pastry rounds filled with vanilla whipped cream
excerpt: Cream puffs are the most accessible choux pastry project: a simple cooked paste piped into rounds, baked until hollow and crisp, then filled with sweetened whipped cream. The entire recipe hinges on getting the choux paste to the right consistency before piping.
sourceNotes: Choux pastry (pate a choux) is documented in French culinary literature from the eighteenth century; the basic formula of water, butter, flour, and eggs is recorded in public-domain cookbooks including Menon's La Cuisiniere Bourgeoise (1746). The filled cream puff form became widespread in home baking through the nineteenth century.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze the unfilled shells once cool. Crisp from frozen at 180 degrees C (conventional) for 5 minutes. Fill once cooled.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The shells can be baked up to 6 hours ahead and kept at room temperature in a dry place. Fill just before serving; filled puffs go soft within 2 to 3 hours.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan: 180 degrees C; do not open the oven in the first 20 minutes

glossaryTerms.definition: Choux paste is ready to pipe when it is smooth, glossy, and falls from a spoon or spatula in a thick V-shape: the paste should drop slowly and hold a point at the tip for a second before falling. If it is too stiff, it will pipe unevenly and may crack badly in the oven. If it is too loose, the puffs will spread flat rather than puffing. The consistency is adjusted by adding eggs gradually; the final egg or half-egg is always added carefully.

content.text: Choux pastry is cooked twice: once on the hob as you beat flour into boiling water and butter to form a paste, and once in the oven where steam trapped inside the paste forces it to puff up hollow. The only real difficulty is judging the
before piping: too thick and the puffs will crack, too thin and they will spread. Add the eggs one at a time and stop when the paste drops from the spatula in a slow, glossy ribbon.

Preheat the oven to 200 degrees C (conventional). Line two baking trays with baking paper. Bring the water, butter, salt, and sugar to a rolling boil in a medium saucepan. Remove from the heat, tip in all the flour at once, and beat vigorously with a wooden spoon until the mixture forms a smooth ball that pulls cleanly from the sides of the pan. Return to a low heat and beat for another minute to drive off excess moisture. Remove from the heat and leave to cool for 5 minutes. Add the beaten eggs a little at a time, beating well after each addition, until the paste is smooth, glossy, and falls from the spoon in a slow, thick ribbon. You may not need all of the egg.

Transfer the paste to a piping bag fitted with a plain 1 cm nozzle. Pipe rounds about 4 cm in diameter onto the lined trays, spacing them 5 cm apart. Smooth any peaks with a damp finger. Bake for 25 to 30 minutes without opening the oven, until the puffs are deep golden brown and feel light and hollow when tapped. Remove from the oven, pierce each one with a skewer to release steam, and leave to cool on a rack. The shells must be completely cool before filling.

Whip the cold cream with the icing sugar and vanilla to soft peaks. Split each shell in half horizontally. Fill the bottom half generously with whipped cream, replace the lid, and dust with icing sugar if you like. Serve within 2 hours.
